title: Today's AI Brief: Amazon develops a warehouse robot workers can speak to
summary: A look at the latest AI news, including Amazon's new warehouse robot and Microsoft's AI coworker

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to today's AI brief. I've got a couple of interesting stories to share with you from the world of artificial intelligence. First up, Amazon has developed a new warehouse robot that workers can actually speak to. Now, I know what you're thinking - robots taking over the world - but hear me out. This one's a bit different.

According to The Verge, Amazon's new robot, called Proteus, is designed to support warehouse workers, not replace them. It's a big deal, as it marks a shift towards more collaborative robots that can interact with humans in a more natural way. I'm looking at this and thinking it's a pretty cool application of AI - who wouldn't want a robot that can understand voice commands? It's like having a personal assistant, but one that's designed to help with tasks in a warehouse setting.

Another story that caught my eye is Microsoft's new AI agent, Scout. As Wired reports, Scout is an always-on AI that can go through your work messages, calendar, and email inbox. It's like having a coworker that never logs off - and never takes a coffee break, for that matter. I'm not sure if that's a good thing or a bad thing, but it's definitely an interesting development. And then there's the fact that it's built into Microsoft Teams, which means it could potentially change the way we work together.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
